summary: Apple released speed-bumped MacBook Pros with 8th and 9th-gen Intel processors, bringing 8 cores to a Mac laptop for the first time. Beyond the CPU updates, Apple quietly tweaked the butterfly keyboard mechanism with new materials aimed at improving reliability, while extending the keyboard service program to cover all butterfly keyboard models including the ones released that day. Gruber sees these updates as evidence that Apple has refocused on the Mac after years of neglect, noting the aggressive hardware update cadence. He also reads the tea leaves on the upcoming Mac Pro, suggesting Apple's silence about WWDC hardware likely means the Mac Pro will appear at the keynote.
key_points: The 15-inch MacBook Pro gets the most significant upgrades, with an 8-core option hitting 5 GHz Turbo Boost for the first time in an Apple portable

Apple made a stealth material change to the butterfly keyboard mechanism to address stuck keys and double-typing, while adding all butterfly keyboards to the service program — an implicit admission the previous keyboards weren't good enough
The pre-WWDC timing was strategic: speed bumps don't warrant stage time, and Apple doesn't want to discuss keyboard reliability problems in a keynote
Regular speed bumps signal Apple's renewed commitment to the Mac after years where the lack of updates led many to conclude the company was losing interest

Gruber's subjective keyboard complaints — low travel, full-size arrow keys, no hardware Esc — all share a common thread: they make keyboards look better but work worse, contradicting Jobs's 'design is how it works' axiom
money_quotes: One word that doesn't appear in today's announcement is 'keyboard'. Seriously, when the announcement went live at 1pm ET, the first thing I did was search for 'keyboard': 'Not found'.

At the very least, talking about this material tweak and including all butterfly keyboard models in the service program is an acknowledgement that last year's keyboards were not good enough. That was the worst case scenario — that Apple didn't see a problem.
Last year, a source at Apple admitted to me that they had 'taken their eye off the ball on Mac'. Regular speed bumps are a very strong sign that their eye is back on the ball.

These things all make the keyboards look better but work worse. That, of course, is in direct contradiction to the well-known Steve Jobs axiom: 'It's not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.'
I wrote 'just speed bumps' a few paragraphs ago, but speed bumps are important in the pro market.
The lack of speed bumps in recent years naturally led some to conclude that Apple, institutionally, was losing interest in the Mac.
If we want to cover all our bases, there's a fourth possible state for the new Mac Pro: scrapped. Off to a grave next to AirPower in the 'Announced but Never Shipped' cemetery.
key_insight: Apple's quiet keyboard material tweak and inclusion of all butterfly models in the service program is a tacit admission of failure that matters more than the CPU speed bumps, because it signals Apple finally acknowledges the problem rather than ignoring it.
